coredata.dc:title: A multi-color carbon quantum dots based on the coordinated effect of quantum size and surface defects with green synthesis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Youjun
Yu, Lixin
Deng, Yakun
Peng, Kangliang
Yu, Ying
Zeng, Xiaoling
coredata.dc:description: As an important complement to traditional quantum dots (QDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess the characteristics of low toxicity, easy preparation, low cost, and good biocompatibility. Here, we have successfully prepared blue, yellow-green, yellow and red emitting CQDs by a one-step solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine as a precursor and only water and ethanol as solvents. It is noted that the diameter of CQDs satisfies the quantum size effect, which is also corroborated by the reduced graphitization. The decay processes of CQDs obey double exponential fit. The x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) conclusively determines that the red-shift from yellow-green to red CQDs is associated with the carbon-/oxygen-related surface defects except for the quantum size effect. Intriguingly, multi-color CQDs-based LEDs devices using thermoset epoxy resins have been successfully fabricated, which illustrates the great potential of CQDs for future applications in the luminescence field due to their superior performance and environmentally friendly fabrication.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Quantum size effect
Surface defects
LEDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Top, bottom are pictures of b-, yg-, y- and r-CDs (from left to right) under daylight and UV light, respectively. (b) The normalized PL emission spectra of b-, yg-, y- and r-CDs at 360 nm excitation, with maxima at 450, 540, 570 and 630 nm.
Absorption spectra, excitation spectra and emission spectra of (a) b-, (b) yg-, (c) y- and (d) r-CDs.
TEM, HRTEM images and size distribution diagrams of (a) b-, (b) yg-, (c) y- and (d) r-CDs.
XPS survey spectra of the four CDs.

(a) Carbon content and sp2 carbon bonding ratios of the yg-, y-, r-CDs. (b) Oxygen content and C–O bonding ratios of the yg-, y-, r-CDs. (c) Nitrogen content, pyridinic N and pyrrolic N bonding ratios of the yg-, y-, r-CDs.
Photographs (a), CIE coordinates (b) and emission spectra (c) of operating b-, yg-, y-, and r-LED.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of multicolor CDs based on o-phenylenediamine.

head.ce:title: A multi-color carbon quantum dots based on the coordinated effect of quantum size and surface defects with green synthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Materials, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Materials, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Physics and Material, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Road, Nanchang, 330031, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an important complement to traditional quantum dots (QDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess the characteristics of low toxicity, easy preparation, low cost, and good biocompatibility. Here, we have successfully prepared blue, yellow-green, yellow and red emitting CQDs by a one-step solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine as a precursor and only water and ethanol as solvents. It is noted that the diameter of CQDs satisfies the quantum size effect, which is also corroborated by the reduced graphitization. The decay processes of CQDs obey double exponential fit. The x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) conclusively determines that the red-shift from yellow-green to red CQDs is associated with the carbon-/oxygen-related surface defects except for the quantum size effect. Intriguingly, multi-color CQDs-based LEDs devices using thermoset epoxy resins have been successfully fabricated, which illustrates the great potential of CQDs for future applications in the luminescence field due to their superior performance and environmentally friendly fabrication.

ce:section.ce:para: QDs are normal semiconductor structures that are nanoscale in all three dimensions. QDs are mostly composed of metallic elements, such as CdS [1], CdTe [2], ZnSe [3], PbS [4] and PbSe [5] quantum dots. However, these types of QDs have many obvious drawbacks, including the disadvantages of being expensive, highly toxic, inconvenient to handle, and environmentally unfriendly [6]. CQDs, as a new candidate for traditional QDs, well compensate for the above deficiencies, especially low toxicity to biological cells and good compatibility, thus possessing advantages in bio-imaging that are difficult to reach with previous QDs [7–10].

CQDs have exhilarated photoluminescent (PL) properties, and to date there is extensive literature on the preparation of CQDs with emission peaks across the visible light range [11–15]. Some CQDs have an excitation-dependent nature and successfully realize the desire for multiple emission [13,16,17], but there are some problems, such as multiple emission of LEDs based CQDs is difficult to realize due to the fixed excitation wavelength of the LEDs chips. Therefore, one is more than willing to tune the parameters for the purpose of changing the emission wavelength of CQDs. Currently, the main methods to tune the emission peaks are changing the reaction temperature [18], reaction time [19], doping with heteroatoms [20], varying the solvent [18,21], etc. At the same time, it poses some problems, for example, when organic solvents are used, the purification process is not only very tedious, but also uses new organic substances, which is not environmentally friendly. Silica gel column chromatography is one of the commonly used purification methods for CQDs. While it can separate multi-color emitting CQDs, it is very hard to test one's patience and difficult to produce on a large scale [18,20,22]. Consequently, the search for a green and easily purified multicolored emitting CQDs is extremely urgent.

Studying the PL redshift mechanism of CQDs has been a hot topic of research, with the prevailing claims being either quantum size effects or surface defects of CQDs [21–24]. For example, Yuan et al. prepared multicolor fluorescent CQDs based on quantum confinement effects by varying the reaction time, precursor ratio, and adding a small amount of sulfuric acid [19]. Li et al. prepared multicolor CQDs by varying the type of organic solvent and showed that the modulated emission wavelength was controlled by nitrogen/oxygen-related surface defects [21]. To the best of our knowledge, there have been few reports mentioning the existence of a coordinated interaction between the two, which together modulate the PL fluorescence of CQDs.

Herein, multicolor CQDs prepared by green one-step solvothermal method using o-Phenylenediamine (oPD) as precursor. As shown in Scheme 1 , in the preparation process only water and ethanol are used as solvents, which greatly reduces the difficulty of subsequent purification and pollution to the environment. It is obvious that the synthesis process is green and environmental protection. To clarify the red-shift mechanism of the as-fabricated blue (b-CDs), yellow-green (yg-CDs), yellow (y-CDs) and red (r-CDs) CQDs, the transmission electron microscope (TEM), XPS, Raman spectra and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) are measured. Finally, we successfully fabricated multicolor LEDs devices using the four CQDs.

The prepared CQDs are progressively darkened from b-to r-CDs under daylight (Fig. 1 a, top). Meanwhile, the samples exhibit bright fluorescence when exposed to a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1a, bottom), and the corresponding spectral peak positions are shown in Fig. 1b. In addition, the CIE coordinates of b-to r-CDs are marked in Fig. S1, indicating that the blue, green, yellow-green and red emission are obtained for the b-, g-, yg- and r-CDs, respectively. Further investigating optical properties of the CQDs, the PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S2. In contrast to the previously reported literature, the PL emission peak of four prepared CQDs exhibit excitation-independent features. The quantum yields (QY) of b-, yg-, y- and r-CDs are 38.63%, 20.60%, 2.97% and 27.02%, respectively.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and excitation spectra of four CDs are displayed in Fig. 2 . An absorption band located at about 282 nm for four CQDs can be found in the range of 200–350 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CN bond [22,25]. The maximum excitation wavelengths of four CQDs coincide with the absorption peaks in the lower-energy region, indicating that the fluorescence emission originates in the lower-energy band absorption region. Furthermore, both b-CDs and yg-CDs have absorption peaks at 370 nm, suggesting that they have similar surface states and that the red shift from b-CDs to yg-CDs may be assigned to quantum size effects [22]. The absorption bands of yg-(b-), y- and r-CDs are at 370 nm, 415 nm and 560 nm, respectively, demonstrating that they have different surface states [22].

TEM images reveal the variation of the size and morphology of four CQDs (Fig. 3 ). The b-, yg-, y- and r-CDs exhibit sphere shape with good dispersion, and the measured mean particle diameters is 2.95, 3.43, 3.72 and 3.80 nm, respectively. As can be seen in the high-resolution TEM image (HRTEM), four CQDs have a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite, demonstrating the formation of highly crystalline graphitic carbon [26]. It is noteworthy that the lattice fringes of r-CDs are much fuzzier compared to the other three CQDs, predicting a worse crystallinity of r-CDs. Further investigation of the PL emission wavelength versus diameter grain size for the four CQDs is presented in Fig. S3. This result intuitively presents a good linear relation. The above results suggest that the redshifts from b-to r-CDs are affected by quantum size effects based on the TEM results. One detail that should be noted is that the diameter grain sizes of the b-CDs differ significantly from the remaining three CQDs, while the yg-to r-CDs are similar in size, suggesting that there may be other reasons for the redshift of the yg-to r-CDs.

The graphitized structures of the four CQDs are reflected in the Raman spectra (Fig. S4a). The 1380 and 1585 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum represent disordered structures or defects (D band) and graphitic carbon domains (G band), respectively [19,21]. The intensity ratios ID/IG for b-, yg-, y- and r-CDs are 0.76, 1.16, 1.29 and 5.06, respectively. The increase in the ID/IG ratio demonstrates that the amorphous structures and defects are closely associated with the red-shift from b-to r-CDs [22]. This result is consistent with the TEM result. In particular, the high ID/IG ratio of r-CDs corresponds to the blurred lattice fringes in Fig. 3d. FT-IR spectra (Fig. S4b) reveal that the surfaces of the four CQDs possess an abundance of functional groups. The peaks at 2931-3425 cm−1 are observed and assigned to O–H/N–H. In addition, carboxylic CO at 1660 cm−1, sp2 CC at 1462 cm−1, C–N at 1267 cm−1, and C–O at 1089 cm−1, demonstrate the formation of polyaromatic structures of the four CQDs [21,22,25].

To investigate the excited-state radiative relaxation kinetics of the four CQDs, time-resolved PL decay curves and lifetime are measured (Fig. S5 and Table S1). The average fluorescence lifetimes (τ) from b-to r-CDs are 6.34, 2.42, 1.62 and 0.63 ns, respectively, obtained by biexponential fitting. The decay process consists of the faster component τ1, representing the radiative recombination of the eigenstates, and the slower component τ2, attributed to the non-radiative composite due to surface defects [21,25]. It is noted from Table S1 that except for b-CDs, from yg-to r-CDs the percentage of τ2 is decreased from 95.07% to 85.67%, and such high proportion of nonradiative decay indicates that the red shift from yg-to r-CDs is related to defects caused by changes of the surface functional groups of CQDs. To further study surface state, the full spectroscopy XPS is measured, shown in Fig. 4 and the relevant elemental contents are listed in Table S2. There are three typical peaks at 285, 400 and 531 eV assigned to C 1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. Looking at the peak intensities, it is not difficult to find a certain degree of significant increase in O element content from yg-to r-CDs. As shown in Table S2, the content of N elements is essentially constant, while the content of C elements and O elements decreases and increases from yg-to r-CDs, respectively. The O/C ratio increases from 0.11 to 0.42 for yg-, y-, and r-CDs, indicating that the surface states inducing the redshift are caused by defects associated with changes in O and C elemental content.

High-resolution XPS spectra are employed to hunt for more details (Fig. S6). The C1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks encompassing the sp2 carbon (CC/C–C, 284.4 eV), sp3 carbon (C–O/C–N, 286 eV) and carbonyl groups (CO, 287.6 eV) [21], respectively. The N1s band can be deconvoluted into two peaks, corresponding to pyridinic N (398.4 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.2 eV) [22]. The O1s band has two component peaks at 531.5 eV and 532.5 eV, representing CO and C–O, respectively [25]. The XPS data of C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra from yg-, y-to r-CDs all display a certain regularity (Table S3). For example, the gradual decrease of the sp2 carbon content from yg-, y-, to r-CDs indicates a reduction in the graphitization of the surface structure of the CQDs, apparently as a result of the increase of the surface defect sites [21]. The C1s spectra data further confirm the results of Raman spectra. Fig. 5 a straightforwardly shows that both the total carbon content and the sp2 carbon ratio are decreased from yg-to r-CDs. Meanwhile, the total oxygen content is gradually increases from yg-to r-CDs, and the trend keeps in line with the change of C–O ratio (Fig. 5b). The above phenomena are sufficient to demonstrate that the redshift from yg-to r-CDs is closely related to the carbon/oxygen related surface defect sites. It is not difficult to reveal that the conversion of pyridinic N to pyrrolic N from yg-to r-CDs occurred with no significant change in nitrogen content (Fig. 5c). In summary, the redshift mechanism of b-to r-CDs is gradually becoming clear, with the quantum size effect being larger for the b-CDs than for the remaining three CQDs, while the yg-to r-CDs are more influenced by surface defects.

The four CQDs maintain long-term photostability under continuous UV irradiation for 6 h (Fig. S7), implying that the CQDs-based LEDs have the possibility of application. Multi-color LEDs are constructed by encapsulating CQDs in thermoset epoxy on a UV chip. The photographs of b-, yg-, y- and r-LEDs devices are shown in Fig. 6 a and b. The corresponding CIE coordinates are (0.22, 0.30), (0.34, 0.47), (0.47, 0.48) and (0.60, 0.35), respectively. The emission spectra of LEDs cover the whole range of visible light, so such LEDs devices have great potential applications. Comparing the emission spectra of b-, yg, y- and r-CDs, the b-LED emission appears redshifted due to some energy transfer, while the yg- and r-LEDs appear somewhat blueshifted, which is attributed to low concentration loading [27,28].

In conclusion, we have successfully obtained CQDs capable of emitting blue, yellow-green, yellow, and red fluorescence, respectively, by a simple one-step solvothermal method. Since only water and ethanol are used as solvents, the difficulty of subsequent purification of the crude solution is greatly reduced, and it is also more environmentally friendly. TEM and Raman spectroscopy results demonstrate that the four CQDs obey the quantum size effects. The relatively similar sizes of yg, y, and r-CDs make the mechanism at redshift still questionable. The double exponential decay and XPS results confirm that the redshifts for yg, y, and r-CDs are indeed affected by carbon/oxygen related surface defects. Importantly, the blue, green, and yellow-green emission CQDs and the corresponding LED devices are realized based on the coordinated effect of quantum size effects and surface defects. These present results imply that CQDs have promising applications in the field of luminescence and can be prepared with mass production.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers No. 51762033), and Student Innovation Special Funds of Jiangxi province (grant numbers No. YC2021–B027).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2023.02.025.
